coredata.dc:title: Bio-waste derived multifunctional carbon dots for white light generation, forensic and antibacterial applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Varghese, Meera
Jayaram, Saranya
Sarojini, Suma
Kumbhakar, Partha
Balachandran, Manoj
coredata.dc:description: The synthesis of multi-colour emitting solid-state fluorescent (SSF) carbon dots (CDs) is a challenging task due to the phenomenon of aggregation-induced self-quenching. However, this study presents an efficacious method to synthesize CDs from the sap stain of the cupressus lusitanica tree (cl-CDs) via a simple one-step microwave treatment. The resulting SSF CDs exhibited a particle size of approximately 3 nm, high stability, and remarkable efficacy in light conversion when coated on a UV light emitting diode (UV LED). The ensuing coating generated white light with CIE colour coordinates of (0.33, 0.34) and a high luminescence efficiency of approximately 671 L/W. The fluorescence capabilities exhibited by the cl-CDs in response to 254 nm and 365 nm UV light excitation make them an ideal choice for developing fluorescent inks to prevent counterfeiting. Moreover, the study investigated the notable fluorescence properties of cl-CDs as a luminescent fingerprint powder for the recognition of latent fingerprints on various surfaces. Additionally, the antibacterial potential of cl-CDs was evaluated against gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria, where the cl-CDs were utilized as an antibacterial dusting powder for fluorescent imaging of latent fingerprints on different substrates. Therefore, we believe that our present work offers a plethora of exciting possibilities for the multifunctional application of SSF green CDs with significant implications in white light generation, counterfeiting prevention, forensic applications, and healthcare.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green synthesis
Carbon dots
Solid-state fluorescence
Latent fingerprint
White light-emitting diode

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Development and imaging of LFPs using cl-CDs
Antibacterial susceptibility test by well diffusion method
Preparation and observation of fluorescent ink patterning
cl-CDs As color-converting layer material in WLED
Visualization of latent fingerprints using cl-CDs
Evaluation of antibacterial activity of cl-CDs against gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge the Centre for Research, and central instrumentation facility (CIF), CHRIST (Deemed to be University) for research fund file no.: SMSS-2221 and for providing necessary instrumentation facilities for successful completion of the work. The authors also acknowledge DST-FIST for the research fund SR/FST/PS-I/2022/208(C). The authors also like to express their gratitude to SAIF-STIC, CUSAT (Kerala) and SAIF-MGU (Kerala) for their support in characterization studies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) schematic illustration of the synthesis of cl-cds having fluorescence in solid state and colloidal form, and have potential application in the fabrication of wled, fingerprint imaging and bacterial inactivation. b) eds analysis of cl-cds, c) tem image of cl-cds. histogram of particle size distribution is shown in insets. d) hr-tem image of cl-cds.Fourier transform (FFT) image is presented in the upper inset. Lower inset shows the lattice spacing with 0.22 nm obtained from the lattice image.

PL spectra and excitation-emission matrices of cl-CDs dispersed in (a,d) Ethanol, (b, e) Acetic acid and (c, f) IPA excited from 300 to 500 nm in steps of 10 nm. (g) Digital photographs of cl-CDs dispersed in different solvents and h) SSF cl-CDs under the exposure of 254 nm light, 365 nm light and day light. i) Digital photographs of Hand drawn patterns and handwritten letters using cl-CDs fluorescent ink on a glass slide under 254 nm light, 365 nm light and day light. j) Deconvoluted solid-state PL spectra of cl-CDs corresponding to 250 nm excitation.

a) Light emission spectra of WLED and top and side views of the WLED in insets. b) CIE chromaticity diagram of WLED. c) Emission intensity of WLED with illumination time up to 36 h. d) Variation in Emission intensity of the WLED with increasing injection current. e) Integrated area under the emission curves of WLED with varying injection currents. f) Emission intensity of WLED with different applied temperatures. g) Dispersion of light from WLED as it passes through a prism, h) Christ university logo illuminated by the fabricated WLED and the source light (inset).

a) Schematic illustration of the method of developing fluorescent fingerprint imaging with the aid of cl-CDs. b) Identification of different levels of fingerprint features. c) Gray level intensity profile corresponding to the fingerprint obtained on a chart paper (inset). The digital photographs of the well diffusion test of cl-CDs dispersed in water (c1), Ethanol (c2), DMF (c3) and DMSO (c4) conducted on E. coli (d) and S. aureus(e) bacteria. Ampicillin (AMP) was used as positive control. f) Zone of inhibitions of cl-CDs observed with the tested bacteria. g) Angle of contact of the sample in ethanol studied on a glass substrate.

LED, fluorescent ink, Fingerprint imaging, Antibacterial

head.ce:title: Bio-waste derived multifunctional carbon dots for white light generation, forensic and antibacterial applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and Electronics, CHRIST (Deemed to be University), Bengaluru, Karnataka 560029, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics and Electronics, CHRIST (Deemed to be University), Bengaluru, Karnataka 560029, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Life Sciences, CHRIST (Deemed to be University), Bengaluru, Karnataka 560029, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Life Sciences, CHRIST (Deemed to be University), Bengaluru, Karnataka 560029, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The synthesis of multi-colour emitting solid-state fluorescent (SSF) carbon dots (CDs) is a challenging task due to the phenomenon of aggregation-induced self-quenching. However, this study presents an efficacious method to synthesize CDs from the sap stain of the cupressus lusitanica tree (cl-CDs) via a simple one-step microwave treatment. The resulting SSF CDs exhibited a particle size of approximately 3 nm, high stability, and remarkable efficacy in light conversion when coated on a UV light emitting diode (UV LED). The ensuing coating generated white light with CIE colour coordinates of (0.33, 0.34) and a high luminescence efficiency of approximately 671 L/W. The fluorescence capabilities exhibited by the cl-CDs in response to 254 nm and 365 nm UV light excitation make them an ideal choice for developing fluorescent inks to prevent counterfeiting. Moreover, the study investigated the notable fluorescence properties of cl-CDs as a luminescent fingerprint powder for the recognition of latent fingerprints on various surfaces. Additionally, the antibacterial potential of cl-CDs was evaluated against gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria, where the cl-CDs were utilized as an antibacterial dusting powder for fluorescent imaging of latent fingerprints on different substrates. Therefore, we believe that our present work offers a plethora of exciting possibilities for the multifunctional application of SSF green CDs with significant implications in white light generation, counterfeiting prevention, forensic applications, and healthcare.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: CDs synthesized through an environmentally friendly process have emerged as a promising alternative to inorganic quantum dots due to their manifold benefits. These fluorescent materials have demonstrated exceptional biocompatibility and suitability, making them a nascent class of materials with vast potential applications in various fields, including medical, forensic, and optoelectronics [1–3]. Notably, the low toxicity profile and biocompatibility of carbon dots have sparked particular interest within the medical and forensic industries.

Among the currently available solid-state lighting technologies, White light emitting diode (WLED) using different phosphors as packaging material on InGaN LED chip is popular for white lighting [4]. However, problems related to reduced thermal stability, color purity, and operating lifetime associated with these devices limit their applications. Therefore, the invention of new light-emitting material that can subdue the aforementioned limitations of LEDs is of great research interest. The introduction of quantum dots into LED technology has brought remarkable progress to the field with their high yield, good stability, excellent luminescence efficiency, and multicolor emission capability [5,6]. As emerging light conversion materials, CDs synthesized from a variety of precursors via diverse synthesis methods were enormously tried in the fabrication of WLEDs [7].

Owing to their good control over spectral composition, compact size that helps easy maneuvering, and potential for digital integration, WLEDs have created novel avenues in biomedical applications. However, bacterial infections associated with medical devices is a major threat to the therapeutical safety [8]. Therefore, it is imperative to employ biocompatible and antibacterial packaging materials for WLEDs to prevent bacterial adhesion and colonization. CDs are widely recognized for their intrinsic antibacterial properties that can effectively combat a broad range of bacteria and they are less toxic to mammalian cells. Furthermore, CDs have a decreased risk of developing antibiotic resistance, as they follow a unique antibacterial mechanism. These peculiar characteristics of CDs make them an attractive prospect for the creation of innovative antimicrobial agents, which hold significant potential for combating infections in healthcare facilities. Immense efforts have been taken to investigate the potential application of CDs as antibacterial agents towards different bacterial species [9,10]. Additionally, antibacterial properties of the CDs can be highly creditable when they use as fingerprint powders.

Fingerprints are distinctive features of human individuals that have great forensic value in investigating evidence at any crime scene. Diverse methods have been developed in the past years for the visualization of invisible/latent fingerprints, and among them, powder dusting is a simple and popular method. The powder dusting method demands the interaction of the dusting material with the developer and hence the for this application. However, conventional dusting powders are associated with selectivity issues and high toxicity, and some of them fail on certain relevant forensic surfaces [11]. As highly fluorescent, biocompatible, and low toxic and antibacterial materials, CDs have created significant interest in visualizing latent fingerprints [12,13].

Moreover, CDs have been also experimented for anti-counterfeiting applications. The threat of counterfeit technologies poses a significant risk to human safety, leading to the development of advanced nano anti-counterfeiting systems. Security inks are a crucial solution for safeguarding essential documents and assets. Stimuli-responsive fluorescent CDs have captured much attention due to their ability to emit various fluorescent colors in response to external stimuli. The distinctive security features of CDs make them an arduous target for counterfeiters, while their favorable fluorescent properties make them an excellent choice for invisible ink for security purposes, particularly in printing and writing. This fluorescent ink can also be used commercially in printing technology. The CDs-based fluorescent ink is simple to prepare, cost-effective, and has reliable fluorescent properties, making it a promising contender for anti-counterfeiting printing and writing of confidential documents for security purposes [14,15].

Though several studies have been reported on the synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials and their potential applications in diverse fields, the utility of multifunctional CDs for complementary applications is least explored [16,17]. Herein, a simple and low-cost strategy is introduced for the green synthesis of SSF and multifunctional CDs from a bio-waste material, the sap stain of cupressus lusitanica. Considerable research has been dedicated to the topic of green synthesis for the production of solid-state fluorescent CDs. However, synthesizing these CDs can often prove to be a formidable task, requiring intricate procedures. Our team has made a noteworthy breakthrough in this area by developing an uncomplicated and cost-effective technique for generating solid-state fluorescent CDs using Cupressus lusitanica as the primary material. These CDs are sourced from sustainable biomass and are both secure and easily producible. The obtained CDs were further used for the fabrication of WLED, fingerprint imaging, and counterfeiting applications without any further purification. For white light generation, photometric parameters were studied to analyze the performance of the WLED device. Detailed study on developing latent fingerprints on various substrates was also demonstrated, and corresponding intensity profiles were recorded. The results of the biocompatibility studies indicate that the use of cl-CDs for fingerprint powdering is comparatively safer than the majority of commercially available alternatives. Antibacterial properties of cl-CDs were tested on gram-positive S. aureus and gram-negative E. coli bacteria, and the MIC and MBC values for both bacterial species were calculated as 250 μg/ml and 500 μg/ml, respectively.

Freshly collected sap stain fromthe cupressus lusitanica tree was used as the precursor for synthesizing cl-CDs. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) (MW 115000) granules of AR grade obtained from Research-lab fine chem industries, India was used as the binder for LED preparation. Gram-positive S. aureus and Gram-negative E. coli were obtained from PSG Institute of Medical Sciences, Tamil Nadu, and were subcultured in fresh nutrient agar was used for antibacterial investigation. Luria Bertini broth, Mueller-Hinton (MH) broth, and agar were obtained from HiMedia Laboratories.

The structure and morphology of the obtained CDs were studied using a Jeol/JEM 2100 transmission electron microscope and Thermo Fisher Scientific Apreo S LoVac Scanning electron microscope. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) profile was recorded using a Rigaku-6000 diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was taken using a Shimadzu IR spirit-FTIR spectrophotometer (6600 model). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectrum and emission spectra of White LED were recorded using Ocean Optics UV–visible spectrometer. The photoluminescence studies were carried out using a Horriba fluoro-spectrometer.

The cl-CDs were synthesized by a simple microwave method using a domestic microwave oven. Clean and freshly collected sap stain of cupressus lusitanica in a ceramic crucible was treated at 180 °C for 25 min. The colour of the sample was changed from golden yellow to deep brown. The molten sample was allowed to solidify at room temperature and then scraped into a clean bowl. The obtained particles were then ground into fine powder using a mortar pestle. As shown in Fig. 1 a the obtained cl-CDs exhibiting fluorescence in powder and colloidal form was further used for the WLED, fingerprint imaging and antibacterial applications (Fig. 1a).

Solid-state fluorescence of CDs is a challenging phenomenon that may be hindered by the π-π interactions or by the extreme resonance energy transfer in their aggregate form [18]. This quenching property limits the usage of CDs in many applications like fluorescent ink, fingerprint detection, photoelectric devices, etc. Here, the SSF cl-CDs from the sap stain of cupressus lusitanica tree were used as a light conversion material to generate white light. For the preparation of the coating material, PVA was dissolved in distilled water at 90 °C and after cooling, cl-CDs were dispersed in it by physical mixing. The resultant mixture was coated on a 380 nm UV LED, followed by drying under a laboratory lamp. The coating and drying processes were repeated until a fine, uniform coating was obtained on the LED.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development and imaging of LFPs using cl-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fingerprint impressions were obtained by pressing the thumb against glass and various substrates. To develop the latent fingerprints, cl-CDs were used through a conventional powder-dusting method. A smooth brush was used to pick up cl-CDs powder, which was then uniformly scattered onto the latent fingerprints. The excess powder was gently removed using the brush. Bright green fluorescent patterns of fingerprints were observed under 254 nm light, and pictures were captured using a digital camera.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial susceptibility test by well diffusion method
ce:section.ce:para: S. aureus and E. coli were cultured in Luria Bertini broth at 37 °C for 24 h at 125 rpm agitation. This bacterial culture was used for the initial antibacterial susceptibility test of the samples by the agar well-diffusion method. Overnight cultures of the bacterial strains S. aureus (Gram-positive) and E. coli (Gram-negative bacteria) were spread plated on Mueller-Hinton (MH) agar media (HiMedia Laboratories Pvt.Ltd.). 9 mm diameter wells were made on the media using a sterile cork borer. 100 µL of cl-CDs dispersed in water (C1), ethanol (C2), DMF (C3), and DMSO (C4) were loaded into the wells. The antibiotic, Ampicillin (HiMedia Laboratories Pvt.Ltd.) was used as a positive control for this test. The plates were then incubated at 37 °C for 24 h, and the zone of inhibition was measured in millimeter.

Minimum Inhibitory Concentration (MIC) of the cl-CDs dispersed in DMSO (C4) was calculated by broth dilution method by evaluating the visible growth of bacteria in the broth and also by measuring the optical density at 600 nm (OD600). E. coli and S. aureus (2x 108 CFU mL−1) were incubated with C4 for 16 h under shaking at 125 rpm. Compared to the control, the lowest concentration of C4 that could completely inhibit (>99 %) bacterial growth was reported as the MIC. The MBC values were determined from the MIC values by spread plating 50 μL of culture from the tubes, which did not show any turbidity on Mueller Hinton Agar media in a petri dish and incubating for 20 h at 37 °C. Checking for the presence of bacterial colonies on the MHA media, the lowest concentration of the sample at which no bacterial colonies were observed was taken as MBC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and observation of fluorescent ink patterning
ce:section.ce:para: To create a highly viscous fluorescent ink, 0.5 g of PVA was added to 10 ml of distilled water and mixed using a magnetic stirrer at 90 °C. Then, 2 mg of cl-CDs were added to the PVA gel and mixed thoroughly with the aid of magnetic stirring. The resulting ink was poured into an empty ink pen and used to draw patterns and letters on a glass slide. The slide was then dried and placed under UV light at 254 nm and 365 nm to produce fluorescent images.

The morphology of the synthesized cl-CDs was studied by SEM and TEM analyses. The SEM image of cl-CDs (Fig. S1) indicated the formation of bigger particles through the aggregation of highly amorphous carbon nanoparticles. Fig. 1b displays the EDS analysis of the sample which confirms the presence of carbon (86.24 Atomic %) and Oxygen (13.76 Atomic %) respectively. The TEM image of cl-CDs as shown in Fig. 1c confirms the successful synthesis of dots with homogeneous particle size and quasi-spherical shape from the green source. The size distribution histogram of cl-CDs, showing an average particle size of ∼ 3 ± 1 nm is shown in inset. Fig. 1d shows the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) image of cl-CDs. The semi-crystalline nature of the CDs that have been obtained is evident from the partial crystalline structures that are located at the center. These structures are in contrast to the well-crystalline structures that are present at the edges of the CDs. Furrier transform (FFT) image corresponding to the crystalline structures is presented in the upper inset. The lower inset shows the lattice spacing derived from the lattice image, which demonstrates a value of 0.22 nm. The XRD pattern of the cl-CDs (Fig. S2a) exhibited a prominent peak at ∼ 15° corresponding to crystalline cores with disordered surfaces and broad peaks at ∼ 23.5° and 43.1°, which implies highly disordered carbon structure [19]. FTIR spectrum of cl-CDs indicating the presence of various surface functional groups that possibly contributed to the solid-state emission properties of the sample (Fig. S2b). The characteristic peaks at 3636.86 cm−1 and 2993 cm−1 are belong to O-H stretching of alcohol and C-H stretching in alkane compounds correspondingly [20,21]. At 2349 cm−1 a strong peak formed by O = C = O asymmetric stretching bending vibration is observed [22]. Peaks at 1903.58 cm−1,2095.64 cm−1 and 1519.44 cm−1 are ascribed to C = C = C stretching, N = C = S stretching and N-O stretching, respectively [23]. The C-N stretching of amine caused a peak at 1053.65 cm−1. Finally, peak at 1684.29 cm−1 is attributable to the C = N stretching [24].

The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of cl-CDs in colloid form was studied from 240 nm to 700 nm (Fig. S3). A clear absorption band was observed at ∼273 nm, which is ascribed to C = C carbon core emission arising from the π-π* transition of sp2 conjugated carbon atoms [25,26]. In order to investigate the fluorescent behaviour corresponding to solvent interactions, cl-CDs were dispersed in solvents of varying polarity, including Ethanol (EtOH), Acetic acid (AcOH), IPA, DMSO and DMF and studied their PL properties (Fig. 2 b – f and Fig. S4). The anticipated outcome was a red-shift in the PL emission with increasing solvent polarity, owing to the elevated energy requirements to alter the dipole direction of the solvent molecules in polar solvents. However, the effect of solvent on the emission was not very evident for the cl-CDs. This indicates that carbon-core or surface functional groups do not affect the solvation dynamics. [27,28].

In the PL spectra of cl-CDs in Ethanol (EtOH), the highest emission peak was positioned at ∼490 nm, corresponding to ∼390 nm excitation. The emission peak centers showed a gradual red shift with the excitation wavelength. The PL intensity increased with the excitation at first and then decreased after the maximum emission point. These results confirm the excitation dependent tunable emission properties of cl-CDs. The PL characteristics of cl-CDs dispersed in different solvents such as AcOH, IPA, DMSO, and DMF were also exhibited the same trend.

Both the size effects and surface defects have a significant impact on the electronic structures of CDs, thus controlling their PL mechanism. The particle size distribution of the CDs obtained indicates that they range in size from 2 to 4 nm. The redshift observed in the distinct PL emission as the size increases is due to a reduction in energy gap resulting from π electron delocalization within the sp2 domain. Therefore, the size effect plays a crucial role in the excitation-dependent PL of CDs [29].

The unique characteristics of CDs become particularly apparent when dispersed in various solvents. The functional groups attached to the surface of the CDs are linked to carbon backbones, which not only enhance their solubility in polar solvents, but also introduce a range of surface defects with varying energy levels. This creates a multitude of electronic transition possibilities and diverse PL properties for CDs. When subjected to a specific excitation wavelength, CDs exhibit a distinct type of light emission that is governed by a surface energy trap. However, modulation of the excitation wavelength results in the emergence of other corresponding emissive traps located on the surface of the carbon dots, thereby dominating the photoluminescence mechanism. Consequently, the photoluminescence of carbon dots is significantly influenced by both the size of the carbon dots and the defects present on their surface [30].

The synthesis of SSF CDs is difficult to attain due to their aggregation induced quenching effects in powder form. The cl-CDs powder appeared in a pale yellow colour under visible light and exhibited green and white fluorescence when exposed to UV light of 254 nm and 365 nm respectively. The fluorescence of cl-CDs in colloidal and solid forms under different wavelengths of light is shown in Fig. 2g and h respectively. The reaction between the carboxyl group and the amino group on the surface of cl-CDs might have formed a protective shell that could separate adjacent CDs to avoid quenching effects and consequently, a solid-state fluorescence was observed with the material [31]. Further the cl-CDs dispersed in PVA gel was used as a fluorescent ink to obtain fluorescent letters and patterns on a glass slide. After drying, the glass plate was exposed to UV light to obtain green and white fluorescent characters on it based on the wavelengths of the exposed light (Fig. 2i). The solid-state emission spectra of cl-CDs excited from 250 to 280 nm showed a broad-ranged emission in the visible region (Fig. S5).

Fig. 2j shows the deconvoluted emission spectrum of cl-CDs corresponding to 250 nm excitation. The possible emission components and peaks were identified corresponding to energy levels around 3.2 eV, 2.8 eV, 2.5 eV, and 2.3 eV. To explain the PL emission, an energy band diagram is given in the inset of Fig. 2j, showing the possible π – π* transition and the defect states transitions involved in the emission [41]. For cl-CDs the excitation dependent emission is dominated which results from the trap states originated by the functional groups present on the surface of cl-CDs. Unlike the radiative π – π* transition of sp2 carbon, this excitation dependent emission involves multiple transition modes with diverse energy levels [32]. The structural characteristics of CDs also determines their quenching properties in solid state. Compared to the amorphous CDs, the crystalline CDs have high self-quenching tendency in aggregated state [33]. The Fluorescence lifetime decay of cl-CDs powder was recorded (Fig. S6) and average lifetime of the sample was calculated using the Eq. (1).
(1)
τ
av =
ε
τ
i
A i
ε
τ i
where, τ av is the average fluorescence lifetime, A is amplitude of the lifetime component and τ is the corresponding lifetime value [34]. For cl-CDs the average lifetime was calculated as ∼1.04 ns. Moreover, the luminescence quantum yield of cl-CDs is calculated as 48.73 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: cl-CDs As color-converting layer material in WLED
ce:section.ce:para: Recent progresses in solid-state optoelectronics led to the high demand for LEDs for diverse applications [35–38]. Motivated by the solid-state fluorescence emission of the synthesized CDs, we attempted to fabricate a WLED. For the fabrication of WLED, cl-CDs were mixed with PVA polymer and employed as a color-converting layer on a ∼380 nm UV LED. The preparation of the CDs/polymer composites is detailed in the experimental section. Fig. 3 a shows the emission spectrum of the fabricated WLED that covers the whole visible region (350 to 750 nm).

The top and side views of the fabricated LED are shown in the inset of Fig. 3a. The CIE color coordinates of the fabricated WLED are found to be (0.33, 0.34), which is marked in the CIE chromaticity diagram of WLED (Fig. 3b). Continuous illumination of LEDs may cause degradation of CDs and the consequent reduction of emission intensity may affect their effective application in lighting technology [39]. Hence, the stability of the performance is highly important for LEDs. The fluorescence stability of the fabricated WLED with time was checked by continuous illumination of the device for 36 h. The emission intensity was maintained appreciably, which assures the stability of the device with functioning time (Fig. 3c). The variation of the light emission intensity of the WLED with different injection currents is shown in Fig. 3d. As the current increased, a gradual increase in emission intensity was observed which is very significant at higher currents. Fig. 3e shows the increasing area of the emission curves with increasing current. With the increasing power, the LED device heats, and hence, temperature stability of the fabricated LED has a great role in determining the quality of the device. For that the WLED was heated up to 333 K and the emission intensities corresponding to increasing temperatures were noted in steps of 10 K. From the graphical representation (Fig. 3f), it is clear that, the WLED maintains almost equivalent performance even at increased temperature. Fig. 3g shows the dispersion of the emitted light from the fabricated device into different colour regions when it is transmitted through a prism, confirming the generation of white light. Further confirmation, the colour distinguishability was checked by illuminating the logo with a UV LED source and the fabricated WLED (Fig. 3h). Compared to the source light, the light from the WLED showed a clear logo lightened by white light. The fabricated WLED emitted white light having high luminous efficiency of ∼671.48 L/W which is calculated using the following equation [40].
(2)
L u min o u s
e f f i c a c y
o f
o p t i c a l
r a d i a t i o n =
683
lm
W

P h

P

λ
∫

λ
d
λ
where,
P h
λ
is the spectral distribution of the photopic curve and
P
λ
is that of white light. The maximum luminous efficacy of radiation for monochromatic light at 555 nm, is obtained to be 683 lm/W and is the peak of the photopic curve.

The color coordinate temperature was calculated by McCamy's polynomial formula:
(3)
C o l o r
c o o r d i n a t e
t e m p e r a t u r e = a
n
3
+ b
n
2
+ c n + d
The color temperature of the fabricated WLED was calculated as 5510 K which is in the region of day light [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualization of latent fingerprints using cl-CDs
Evaluation of antibacterial activity of cl-CDs against gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: CDs possess inherent antibacterial properties that can be utilized to inactivate gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria [46]. The antibacterial efficiency of cl-CDs dispersed in water (C1), ethanol (C2), DMF (C3) and DMSO (C4) were studied by checking the growth inhibition inGram-negative (E. coli) and Gram-positive (S. aureus) bacteria. As seen in Fig. 4d and e that depict the digital photographs of the well diffusion test, among the tested samples, Cy4 showed superior antibacterial activity against both tested microorganisms. Fig. 4f gives the graphical representation of the comparison of the zone of inhibition of cl-CDs in different solvents (1 mg/ml concentration) obtained against S. aureus and E. coli bacteria. The MIC and MBC values of the Cy4 sample on E. coli and S. aureus were estimated as 250 µg/ml and 500 μg/ml respectively. As discussed earlier, the antibacterial potential of cl-CDs can be advantageous for their application in preparation of WLEDs to be used for biomedical applications and in latent fingerprint development. Moreover, the angle of contact measurements showed that the contact angle of the sample is 41.58° (Fig. 4g). This indicates the good wettability of the sample which is a desired property for fingerprint imaging applications. For latent fingerprint applications biosafety and antibacterial potential of the material are highly appreciated for the safe handling of the samples and the secure development of images. Similarly, the application of WLED in biomedical sectors also value the antibacterial properties of the coating material to avoid any possible infections during treatment. Owing to their origin from bio material, cl-CDs are eco-friendly and biologically safe towards mammalian cells. A comparative analysis of cl-CDs with previous research efforts within the same domain has been performed (Table 2 ). The results of the analysis demonstrate the promising multifunctional applications of cl-CDs for optoelectronic and forensic purposes. Notably, high quantum yield is an essential characteristic of cl-CDs that makes them particularly suitable for these applications. The findings of this study hold significant implications for the advancement of research on the use of cl-CDs in optoelectronics and forensics.

In summary, we report a facile microwave green synthesis of SSF CDs from the sap stain of cupressus lusitanica for the construction of WLEDs, fingerprint imaging, fluorescent ink and bacterial inactivation. The synthesized cl-CDs at 365 nm excitation exhibited bright white light emission which is utilized for their application as light conversion phosphor on a UVLED for the fabrication of WLED. The fabricated device was exhibited CIE color co-ordinates of (0.33, 0.34) with a color temperature of 5510 K. The luminous efficiency of the device was calculated as ∼671 L/W. The fluorescence stability of the fabricated LED was checked up to 36 h of continuous illumination and temperature of 333 K which showed the excellent stability of the device. Additionally, the cl-CDs offered green fluorescence under 254 nm excitation which was used for the fluorescent imaging of latent fingerprints on different forensically important substrates. The developed latent fingerprint images showed high contrast and great resolution with low background interference. Interestingly they are also capable of inactivating gram-positive S. aureus and gram-negative E. coli bacteria with MIC and MBC values of 250 µg/ml and 500 µg/ml, respectively. Hence, we believe, the as synthesized bio-waste derived SSF cl-CDs can be a potential candidate for bio-medical and forensic applications with their white light generation capability, finger print imaging proficiency and antibacterial properties.

Meera Varghese: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Saranya Jayaram: Methodology, Investigation. Suma Sarojini: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Partha Kumbhakar: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Manoj Balachandran: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the Centre for Research, and central instrumentation facility (CIF), CHRIST (Deemed to be University) for research fund file no.: SMSS-2221 and for providing necessary instrumentation facilities for successful completion of the work. The authors also acknowledge DST-FIST for the research fund SR/FST/PS-I/2022/208(C). The authors also like to express their gratitude to SAIF-STIC, CUSAT (Kerala) and SAIF-MGU (Kerala) for their support in characterization studies.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2023.115456.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
